findings.one_line: In an LS-ADMMG, S→A_0 is labelled {A_0}; each eligible A_0-serious context deletes it (as well as A_0↔Y_0), so Y_0 is not a descendant of S, the asserted line-7 separation failure vanishes, and {Y_0} is reachable by fixing S rather than reaching line 9.

Its claimed I_0∈C_cs2 imports the false terminal trace: with the required selector-edge label, SS-ID has a line-8 candidate, so this proof does not establish the displayed counterexample or its certificate-emptiness consequence.
The asserted four-vertex line-9 input is invalidated by the forced deletion of S→A_0 in every A_0-serious candidate context, so the claimed incompleteness witness is not established.

Although U is an input, the certificate constraints never require the skeleton's selector law to have support U (or the specified π); the later contradiction applies support-pooling to certificate models without this required premise.

This defines the context-setting interventional law p(X_Y|do(A=a,S=empty)), but the core target_estimand/estimand_functional instead use a natural-selector joint event and an extra factor π(empty); the parity and support-pooling proofs establish only the former.

cited_checks.note: The fetched PMLR paper identifies Algorithm 1 as SS-ID for p(Y(a,S=empty)) and its Theorem 4 states that Algorithm 1 is sound; this matches the node's invocation on hidden-variable CS-SCM LS-ADMMG inputs.

The fetched PMLR paper’s Theorem 5 states non-identification when Algorithm 1 fails at lines 4 or 14, or Algorithm 2 line 3, matching exactly the three enumerated returns and not the final Algorithm 2 return.
